               The Secretary of Labor has determined that
               the publication of this periodical is necessary
               in the transaction of the public business re-
               quired by law of this Department. Use of funds
               for printing this periodical has been approved
               by the Director of the Office of Management
               and Budget through February 28, 1981. Con-
               trolled circulation postage paid at Riverdale,
               Md.

               Unless identified as copyrighted, articles and
               tabulations in this publication are in the public
               domain and may be reproduced without per-
               mission of the Federal Government. Please

               Labor Statistics.

                Developments, April 1980

                A-4. Labor force by sex, age, and race

                                                                                   Total labor force                                                Civilian labor force

               Explanatory Notes




                                                                               Introduction

                 The statistics in this periodical are estimated from two major              dustries is included. The payroll survey covers only wage and salary
               sources: (1) Household interviews, and (2) reports from employers.            employees on the payrolls. of nonagricultural establishments.

                 Data based on household interviews are obtained from a sample               Multiple job holding. The household approach provides information
               survey of the population 16 years of age and over. The survey is con-         on the work status of the population without duplication, since each
               ducted each month by the Bureau of the Census for the Bureau of               person is classified as employed, unemployed, or not in the labor
               Labor Statistics and provides comprehensive data on the labor force,          force. Employed persons holding more than one job are counted only
               the employed and the unemployed, including such characteristics as            once and are classified according to the job at which they worked the
               age, sex, race, family relationship, marital status, occupation, and in-      greatest number of hours during the survey week. In the figures based
               dustry attachment. The survey also provides data on the                       on establishment reports, persons who worked in more than one
               characteristics and past work experience of those not in the labor            establishment during the reporting period are counted each time their
               force. The information is collected by trained interviewers from a            names appear on payrolls.
               sample of about 65,000 households, representing 629 areas in 1,133
               counties and independent cities, with coverage in 50 States and the           Unpaid absences from jobs. The household survey includes among the
               District of Columbia. The data collected are based on the activity or         employed all persons who had jobs but were not at work during the
               status reported for the calendar week including the 12th of the month.        survey week—that is, were not working but had jobs from which they
                                                                                             were temporarily absent because of illness, bad weather, vacation,

               Reliability of the eetlmatee                                                    Sampling error. The standard error is primarily a measure of sampl-
                                                                                           ing variability, that is, of the variation that occurs by chance because a
                 There are two types of errors possible in an estimate based on a           sample rather than the entire population is surveyed. The sample
              sample survey—sampling and nonsampling. The standard errors pro-             estimate and its estimated standard error enables one to construct con-

                                                                                           The "link relative" technique

               from the sample would differ from its benchmark by less than the                                                        level

               root-mean-square error. The chances are about 19 out of 20 that the
               difference would be less than twice the root-mean-square error.

                                                                     Seasonal adjustment

                  Over a course of a year, the size of the Nation's labor force, the          All civilian labor force and unemployment rate statistics, as well as
               levels of employment and unemployment, and other measures of                the major employment and unemployment estimates, are computed
               labor market activity undergo sharp fluctuations due to such seasonal       by aggregating independently adjusted series. For example, for each
               events as changes in weather, reduced or expanded production,               of the three major labor force components—agricultural employment,
               harvests, major holidays, and the opening and closing of schools.           nonagricultural employment, and unemployment—data for four sex-
               Because these seasonal events follow a more or less regular pattern         age groups (males and females under and over 20 years of age) are
               each year, their influence on statistical trends can be eliminated by ad-   separately adjusted for seasonal variation and are then added to derive
               justing the statistics from month to month. These adjustments make it       seasonally adjusted total figures. In order to provide seasonally ad-
               easier to observe the cyclical and other nonseasonal movements in the       justed total employment and civilian labor force estimates, the ap-
               series. In evaluating changes in a seasonally adjusted series, it is im-    propriate series are aggregated. The official unemployment rate for all
               portant to note that seasonal adjustment is merely an approximation         civilian workers is derived by dividing the estimate for total unemploy-
               based on past experience. Seasonally adjusted estimates have a              ment (the sum of 4 seasonally adjusted sex-age component!) by the
               broader margin of possible error than the original data on which they       civilian labor force (the sum of 12 seasonally adjusted sex-age com-
               are based, since they are subject not only to sampling and other errors     ponents).
               but are also affected by the uncertainties of the seasonal adjustment          Revised seasonally adjusted data for selected labor force series bas-
               process itself. Seasonally adjusted series for selected labor force and     ed on the experience through December 1979, new seasonal adjust-
               establishment data are published regularly in Employment and Earn-          ment factors to be used to calculate the overall unemployment rate for
               ings.                                                                       the first 6 months of 1980, and a description of the current seasonal
                  The seasonal adjustment programs used for these series are based         adjustment methodology are published in the January 1980 issue of
               on an adaption of the standard ratio-to-moving average method. They         Employment and Earnings, Revised seasonally adjusted data cover-
               provide for "moving" adjustment factors to take account of changing         ing the entire 5-year revision period for a broader range of labor force
               seasonal patterns. A detailed description of the methods is given in the    series appear in the February 1980 issue of this publication. Many ad-
